title: Service Workers
summary: A service worker is a script that runs in the background, separate from the page.
It can intercept network requests and serve cached responses.
It is what lets a site work offline.
It powers features like offline mode and push notifications.

sections.text: Normally your JavaScript runs only while the page is open. A service worker is different. It is a special script that runs in the background, between your page and the network, and it unlocks abilities a normal page does not have, like working offline.

sections.title: A Script in the Middle
sections.text: A service worker sits between your web app and the network. It can intercept the requests your page makes and decide how to answer them, perhaps from a cache instead of the network. Because it runs separately from the page, it can even work when no page is open.

sections.title: What It Enables
sections.text: This middle position is powerful. By serving cached files when the network is gone, a service worker lets a site keep working offline. It also enables push notifications, messages that reach the user even when your site is not open. Service workers are the engine behind app like web experiences.

media.alt: A service worker sitting between the page and the network, answering from a cache

sections.title: A Receptionist Who Takes Messages
sections.text: A service worker is like a receptionist sitting between visitors and the office. They can answer common questions from a folder on their desk without disturbing anyone, the cache, and they can take messages when the office is closed, push notifications. They handle things even when the main office is empty.

media.alt: A receptionist answering from a desk folder and taking messages after hours

sections.title: How a Service Worker Works
items: The browser registers your service worker script.
It runs in the background, separate from the page.
It can intercept the network requests the page makes.
It may answer from a cache, even offline.
It can receive push notifications when the site is closed.

sections.text: For safety, service workers only run on secure connections. A site must be served over HTTPS to use one, since intercepting requests is powerful enough that it must be protected.

terms.definition: A background script between page and network.
Running separately from the open page.
Catching requests to decide how to answer them.
A message reaching the user when the site is closed.

questions.text: Where does a service worker run?
options: In the background, separate from the page
questions.explanation: A service worker runs in the background, separate from the page.

options: The network requests your page makes
questions.explanation: A service worker can intercept the network requests your page makes.

questions.text: What does it let a site do?
questions.explanation: By serving cached responses, a service worker lets a site work offline.
